records.ety: [LL. degradatio, from degradare: cf. F. dgradation. See Degrade.]

defs: The act of reducing in rank, character, or reputation, or of abasing; a lowering from one's standing or rank in office or society; diminution; as, the degradation of a peer, a knight, a general, or a bishop.
quotes: He saw many removes and degradations in all the other offices of which he had been possessed.

defs: The state of being reduced in rank, character, or reputation; baseness; moral, physical, or intellectual degeneracy; disgrace; abasement; debasement.
quotes: The . . . degradation of a needy man of letters.
Deplorable is the degradation of our nature.
Moments there frequently must be, when a sinner is sensible of the degradation of his state.

defs: Diminution or reduction of strength, efficacy, or value; degeneration; deterioration.
quotes: The development and degradation of the alphabetic forms can be traced.

defs: A gradual wearing down or wasting, as of rocks and banks, by the action of water, frost etc.
The state or condition of a species or group which exhibits degraded forms; degeneration.
quotes: The degradation of the species man is observed in some of its varieties.

defs: Arrest of development, or degeneration of any organ, or of the body as a whole.
